ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 4, 48, 43, 289050, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `project__c` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL
- `currencyisocode` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c  (190 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `opportunity__c` text NULL

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 4, 48, 43, 289053, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 4, 48, 43, 289501, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4f81-a117-7672-b504-eb8a973d0f2e', conversation_id='019f4f81-a117-7672-b504-eb89780d84f6')

data.overview:
### Global Mapping Plan Overview

#### Cross-Table Decisions:
1. **Key Consistency**: All foreign keys (`AccountId`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Project__c`) must reference the Salesforce-style `Id` fields from their respective tables. Join keys are resolved by directly mapping source foreign keys (e.g., `accountid`, `account__c`) to the target `Id` fields.

2. **Enum Normalization**: 
   - `Customer_Tier__c` (Account): Normalize to 'Gold', 'Silver', 'Bronze', 'Platinum'.
   - `Role__c` (Contact): Normalize to 'Decision Maker', 'End User', 'Technical Contact', 'Executive Sponsor'.
   - `Preferred_Language__c` (Contact): Normalize to 'DE', 'EN', 'FR', 'ES', 'IT'.
   - `StageName` (Opportunity): Normalize to the declared enum domain.
   - `Project_Status__c` (Project__c): Normalize to 'Active', 'Completed', 'In Planning', 'On Hold', 'Cancelled'.
   - **Fallback Policy**: Use NULL for unmatched enum values.

3. **Date Handling**: 
   - Parse all date fields (`closedate`, `go_live_date__c`, `warranty_end_date__c`) using `TO_DATE` with format guards for `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, and `MM/DD/YYYY`. Output as ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text.
   - Fallback to NULL for unparseable dates.

4. **Amount Handling**: 
   - Strip currency symbols and text prefixes from `amount` (Opportunity).
   - Handle European separators: remove thousand-separator dots, swap comma to decimal point, then cast to `DOUBLE PRECISION`.
   - Fallback to NULL for unparseable amounts.

5. **Legacy Columns**: 
   - Populate all `Legacy_*__c` columns with the source natural key (e.g., `legacy_customer_id__c`, `id` for contacts, opportunities, projects, and assets).

6. **NOT NULL Constraints**: 
   - Satisfy with meaningful defaults (e.g., 'Unknown' for `Name`, `LastName`, `Project__c.Name`, `Installed_Asset__c.Name`).
   - Use `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text` for `CreatedDate` and `LastModifiedDate`.
   - Default `IsDeleted` to 0 (false).

7. **Text Normalization**: 
   - Use `INITCAP(TRIM())` for names and enum-like fields.
   - Use `LOWER(TRIM())` for email fields.
   - Use `UPPER(TRIM())` for language codes and currency codes.

#### Per-Table Pitfalls:
- **Account**: Ensure `Customer_Tier__c` enum normalization is consistent.
- **Contact**: Validate `AccountId` joins correctly with `Account.Id`.
- **Opportunity**: Handle `amount` and `closedate` parsing carefully.
- **Project__c**: Ensure `Account__c` and `Opportunity__c` reference the correct Salesforce-style `Id` fields.
- **Installed_Asset__c**: Validate `Account__c` and `Project__c` joins correctly with their respective tables.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

    CASE

    TRIM("region__c") AS "Region__c",

    TRIM("billingcity") AS "BillingCity",

    TRIM("legacy_customer_id__c") AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'account') }}

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 5, 19, 51, 994387, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Project__c`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Overview: ### Global Mapping Plan Overview

#### Cross-Table Decisions:
1. **Key Consistency**: All foreign keys (`AccountId`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Project__c`) must reference the Salesforce-style `Id` fields from their respective tables. Join keys are resolved by directly mapping source foreign keys (e.g., `accountid`, `account__c`) to the target `Id` fields.

2. **Enum Normalization**: 
   - `Customer_Tier__c` (Account): Normalize to 'Gold', 'Silver', 'Bronze', 'Platinum'.
   - `Role__c` (Contact): Normalize to 'Decision Maker', 'End User', 'Technical Contact', 'Executive Sponsor'.
   - `Preferred_Language__c` (Contact): Normalize to 'DE', 'EN', 'FR', 'ES', 'IT'.
   - `StageName` (Opportunity): Normalize to the declared enum domain.
   - `Project_Status__c` (Project__c): Normalize to 'Active', 'Completed', 'In Planning', 'On Hold', 'Cancelled'.
   - **Fallback Policy**: Use NULL for unmatched enum values.

3. **Date Handling**: 
   - Parse all date fields (`closedate`, `go_live_date__c`, `warranty_end_date__c`) using `TO_DATE` with format guards for `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, and `MM/DD/YYYY`. Output as ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text.
   - Fallback to NULL for unparseable dates.

4. **Amount Handling**: 
   - Strip currency symbols and text prefixes from `amount` (Opportunity).
   - Handle European separators: remove thousand-separator dots, swap comma to decimal point, then cast to `DOUBLE PRECISION`.
   - Fallback to NULL for unparseable amounts.

5. **Legacy Columns**: 
   - Populate all `Legacy_*__c` columns with the source natural key (e.g., `legacy_customer_id__c`, `id` for contacts, opportunities, projects, and assets).

6. **NOT NULL Constraints**: 
   - Satisfy with meaningful defaults (e.g., 'Unknown' for `Name`, `LastName`, `Project__c.Name`, `Installed_Asset__c.Name`).
   - Use `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text` for `CreatedDate` and `LastModifiedDate`.
   - Default `IsDeleted` to 0 (false).

7. **Text Normalization**: 
   - Use `INITCAP(TRIM())` for names and enum-like fields.
   - Use `LOWER(TRIM())` for email fields.
   - Use `UPPER(TRIM())` for language codes and currency codes.

#### Per-Table Pitfalls:
- **Account**: Ensure `Customer_Tier__c` enum normalization is consistent.
- **Contact**: Validate `AccountId` joins correctly with `Account.Id`.
- **Opportunity**: Handle `amount` and `closedate` parsing carefully.
- **Project__c**: Ensure `Account__c` and `Opportunity__c` reference the correct Salesforce-style `Id` fields.
- **Installed_Asset__c**: Validate `Account__c` and `Project__c` joins correctly with their respective tables.

Join keys: account__c, opportunity__c
- Id <- id (Direct mapping from source `id`.)

- Account__c <- account__c (Direct mapping from source `account__c`. Join with `Account.Id` to ensure consistency.)
- Opportunity__c <- opportunity__c (Direct mapping from source `opportunity__c`. Join with `Opportunity.Id` to ensure consistency.)
- Legacy_Project_ID__c <- id (Use source `id` as the legacy project ID.)
- CreatedDate <- (unspecified) (Use CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text for all rows.)
- LastModifiedDate <- (unspecified) (Use CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text for all rows.)
- IsDeleted <- (unspecified) (Default to 0 (false).)
Notes: `Account__c` and `Opportunity__c` must reference the Salesforce-style `Account.Id` and `Opportunity.Id` respectively. Enum normalization is applied to `Project_Status__c`.

Follow the plan. Produce the dbt model for `Project__c`.", timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 5, 19, 51, 994394, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 5, 19, 51, 994733, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4f9e-24b9-7637-8f38-224e8a33e1a4', conversation_id='019f4f9e-24b9-7637-8f38-224d5c62613c')

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content='```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    p.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(INITCAP(TRIM(p.name)), 'Unknown') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('Active', 'Completed', 'In Planning', 'On Hold', 'Cancelled')
        THEN INITCAP(TRIM(p.project_status__c))
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN p.go_live_date__c
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    p.account__c AS "Account__c",
    p.opportunity__c AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.id AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'project__c') }} p
```

- **`Name`**: Normalized using `INITCAP(TRIM())` with a fallback to `'Unknown'` if NULL.

- **`Legacy_Project_ID__c`**: Populated from `project__c.id` (source natural key).
- **`CreatedDate`**: Defaulted to `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text`.
- **`LastModifiedDate`**: Defaulted to `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text`.
- **`IsDeleted`**: Defaulted to `0` (false).

**Reasoning:**

It normalizes text fields, handles date parsing with format guards, and ensures enum values conform to the specified domain. ```sql

SELECT 
    o.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(INITCAP(TRIM(o.name)), 'Unknown') AS "Name",
    COALESCE(
        CASE 
            WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('Prospecting', 'Qualification', 'Needs Analysis', 'Value Proposition', 'Id. Decision Makers', 'Perception Analysis', 'Proposal/Price Quote', 'Negotiation/Review', 'Closed Won', 'Closed Lost') 
                THEN INITCAP(TRIM(o.stagename))
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        'Prospecting'
    ) AS "StageName",
    CASE 
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{4}\d{2}\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL

    CASE 
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' THEN o.amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN REPLACE(o.amount, ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN REPLACE(REPLACE(o.amount, '.', ''), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[^0-9]+([0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?)$' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(o.amount, '[^0-9.]', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION

    END AS "Amount",
    UPPER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    o.accountid AS "AccountId",

    0 AS "IsDeleted"

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

## Opportunity
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}
SELECT 
    o.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(INITCAP(TRIM(o.name)), 'Unknown') AS "Name",
    CASE 
        WHEN INITCAP(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('Prospecting', 'Qualification', 'Needs Analysis', 'Value Proposition', 'Id. Decision Makers', 'Perception Analysis', 'Proposal/Price Quote', 'Negotiation/Review', 'Closed Won', 'Closed Lost') 
        THEN INITCAP(TRIM(o.stagename)) 
        ELSE NULL 
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE 
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{4}\d{2}\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL 
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE 
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' THEN o.amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN REPLACE(o.amount, ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN REPLACE(REPLACE(o.amount, '.', ''), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[^0-9]+([0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?)$' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(o.amount, '[^0-9.]', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE NULL 
    END AS "Amount",
    UPPER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    o.accountid AS "AccountId",
    o.id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "CreatedDate",

    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} o

messages.raw:
ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.

        THEN TRIM(o.stagename)

    CASE
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')

        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' THEN o.amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN REPLACE(o.amount, ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN REPLACE(REPLACE(o.amount, '.', ''), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[^0-9]+([0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?)$' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(o.amount, '[^0-9.]', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 5, 29, 2, 719388, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Opportunity`.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}
SELECT
    o.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(INITCAP(TRIM(o.name)), 'Unknown') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(o.stagename) IN ('Prospecting', 'Qualification', 'Needs Analysis', 'Value Proposition', 'Id. Decision Makers', 'Perception Analysis', 'Proposal/Price Quote', 'Negotiation/Review', 'Closed Won', 'Closed Lost')
        THEN TRIM(o.stagename)
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{4}\d{2}\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' THEN o.amount::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN REPLACE(o.amount, ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN REPLACE(REPLACE(o.amount, '.', ''), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        WHEN o.amount ~ '^[^0-9]+([0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?)$' THEN REGEXP_REPLACE(o.amount, '[^0-9.]', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Amount",
    UPPER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    o.accountid AS "AccountId",
    o.id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} o
```

Reviewer comments:
The `StageName` fallback to 'Prospecting' for unmatched values may not align with the target schema's intent. If the source data contains NULL or unrecognized values, defaulting to 'Prospecting' could misrepresent the actual stage. Consider using NULL as the fallback for unmatched values to maintain data accuracy. Additionally, the `Amount` field's regex for European separators (`^[0-9]+\[0-9]+,[0-9]+$`) may not cover all edge cases (e.g., spaces as thousand separators). Ensure the regex accounts for all possible formats in the source data.

Overview: ### Global Mapping Plan Overview

#### Cross-Table Decisions:
1. **Key Consistency**: All foreign keys (`AccountId`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Project__c`) must reference the Salesforce-style `Id` fields from their respective tables. Join keys are resolved by directly mapping source foreign keys (e.g., `accountid`, `account__c`) to the target `Id` fields.

2. **Enum Normalization**: 
   - `Customer_Tier__c` (Account): Normalize to 'Gold', 'Silver', 'Bronze', 'Platinum'.
   - `Role__c` (Contact): Normalize to 'Decision Maker', 'End User', 'Technical Contact', 'Executive Sponsor'.
   - `Preferred_Language__c` (Contact): Normalize to 'DE', 'EN', 'FR', 'ES', 'IT'.
   - `StageName` (Opportunity): Normalize to the declared enum domain.
   - `Project_Status__c` (Project__c): Normalize to 'Active', 'Completed', 'In Planning', 'On Hold', 'Cancelled'.
   - **Fallback Policy**: Use NULL for unmatched enum values.

3. **Date Handling**: 
   - Parse all date fields (`closedate`, `go_live_date__c`, `warranty_end_date__c`) using `TO_DATE` with format guards for `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, and `MM/DD/YYYY`. Output as ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text.
   - Fallback to NULL for unparseable dates.

4. **Amount Handling**: 
   - Strip currency symbols and text prefixes from `amount` (Opportunity).
   - Handle European separators: remove thousand-separator dots, swap comma to decimal point, then cast to `DOUBLE PRECISION`.
   - Fallback to NULL for unparseable amounts.

5. **Legacy Columns**: 
   - Populate all `Legacy_*__c` columns with the source natural key (e.g., `legacy_customer_id__c`, `id` for contacts, opportunities, projects, and assets).

6. **NOT NULL Constraints**: 
   - Satisfy with meaningful defaults (e.g., 'Unknown' for `Name`, `LastName`, `Project__c.Name`, `Installed_Asset__c.Name`).
   - Use `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text` for `CreatedDate` and `LastModifiedDate`.
   - Default `IsDeleted` to 0 (false).

7. **Text Normalization**: 
   - Use `INITCAP(TRIM())` for names and enum-like fields.
   - Use `LOWER(TRIM())` for email fields.
   - Use `UPPER(TRIM())` for language codes and currency codes.

#### Per-Table Pitfalls:
- **Account**: Ensure `Customer_Tier__c` enum normalization is consistent.
- **Contact**: Validate `AccountId` joins correctly with `Account.Id`.
- **Opportunity**: Handle `amount` and `closedate` parsing carefully.
- **Project__c**: Ensure `Account__c` and `Opportunity__c` reference the correct Salesforce-style `Id` fields.
- **Installed_Asset__c**: Validate `Account__c` and `Project__c` joins correctly with their respective tables.
Source tables: fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity
Join keys: accountid
- Id <- id (Direct mapping from source `id`.)
- Name <- name (Use INITCAP(TRIM(name)). NOT NULL: fallback to 'Unknown' if NULL.)
- StageName <- stagename (Normalize to enum: 'Prospecting', 'Qualification', 'Needs Analysis', 'Value Proposition', 'Id. Decision Makers', 'Perception Analysis', 'Proposal/Price Quote', 'Negotiation/Review', 'Closed Won', 'Closed Lost'. Use INITCAP(TRIM()) and map variants. NOT NULL: fallback to 'Prospecting' if NULL.)
- CloseDate <- closedate (Parse source `closedate` using TO_DATE with format guards (e.g., 'DD.MM.YYYY', 'YYYYMMDD', 'MM/DD/YYYY'). Output as ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text. Fallback to NULL if unparseable.)
- Amount <- amount (Strip currency symbols and text prefixes. Handle European separators: remove thousand-separator dots, swap comma to decimal point. Cast to `DOUBLE PRECISION`. Fallback to NULL if unparseable.)
- CurrencyIsoCode <- currencyisocode (Direct mapping. Trim whitespace and convert to UPPER.)
- AccountId <- accountid (Direct mapping from source `accountid`. Join with `Account.Id` to ensure consistency.)
- Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c <- id (Use source `id` as the legacy opportunity ID.)
- CreatedDate <- (unspecified) (Use CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text for all rows.)
- LastModifiedDate <- (unspecified) (Use CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text for all rows.)
- IsDeleted <- (unspecified) (Default to 0 (false).)

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 5, 29, 48, 7270, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Project__c`.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}
SELECT
    p.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(INITCAP(TRIM(p.name)), 'Unknown') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(p.project_status__c) IN ('Active', 'Completed', 'In Planning', 'On Hold', 'Cancelled')
        THEN TRIM(p.project_status__c)
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN p.go_live_date__c
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    p.account__c AS "Account__c",
    p.opportunity__c AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.id AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'project__c') }} p
```

Reviewer comments:
The `Project_Status__c` field uses exact matches for enum values, which is correct. However, the `Go_Live_Date__c` parsing logic assumes specific date formats. If the source data contains additional formats (e.g., `DD-MM-YYYY`), these will be missed. Expand the format guards to cover all possible source formats. Additionally, the `Account__c` and `Opportunity__c` fields are directly mapped from `p.account__c` and `p.opportunity__c`, but there is no validation to ensure these reference the correct Salesforce-style `Id` fields from their respective tables. Verify cross-table consistency.

Overview: ### Global Mapping Plan Overview

#### Cross-Table Decisions:
1. **Key Consistency**: All foreign keys (`AccountId`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Project__c`) must reference the Salesforce-style `Id` fields from their respective tables. Join keys are resolved by directly mapping source foreign keys (e.g., `accountid`, `account__c`) to the target `Id` fields.

2. **Enum Normalization**: 
   - `Customer_Tier__c` (Account): Normalize to 'Gold', 'Silver', 'Bronze', 'Platinum'.
   - `Role__c` (Contact): Normalize to 'Decision Maker', 'End User', 'Technical Contact', 'Executive Sponsor'.
   - `Preferred_Language__c` (Contact): Normalize to 'DE', 'EN', 'FR', 'ES', 'IT'.
   - `StageName` (Opportunity): Normalize to the declared enum domain.
   - `Project_Status__c` (Project__c): Normalize to 'Active', 'Completed', 'In Planning', 'On Hold', 'Cancelled'.
   - **Fallback Policy**: Use NULL for unmatched enum values.

3. **Date Handling**: 
   - Parse all date fields (`closedate`, `go_live_date__c`, `warranty_end_date__c`) using `TO_DATE` with format guards for `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, and `MM/DD/YYYY`. Output as ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text.
   - Fallback to NULL for unparseable dates.

4. **Amount Handling**: 
   - Strip currency symbols and text prefixes from `amount` (Opportunity).
   - Handle European separators: remove thousand-separator dots, swap comma to decimal point, then cast to `DOUBLE PRECISION`.
   - Fallback to NULL for unparseable amounts.

5. **Legacy Columns**: 
   - Populate all `Legacy_*__c` columns with the source natural key (e.g., `legacy_customer_id__c`, `id` for contacts, opportunities, projects, and assets).

6. **NOT NULL Constraints**: 
   - Satisfy with meaningful defaults (e.g., 'Unknown' for `Name`, `LastName`, `Project__c.Name`, `Installed_Asset__c.Name`).
   - Use `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text` for `CreatedDate` and `LastModifiedDate`.
   - Default `IsDeleted` to 0 (false).

7. **Text Normalization**: 
   - Use `INITCAP(TRIM())` for names and enum-like fields.
   - Use `LOWER(TRIM())` for email fields.
   - Use `UPPER(TRIM())` for language codes and currency codes.

#### Per-Table Pitfalls:
- **Account**: Ensure `Customer_Tier__c` enum normalization is consistent.
- **Contact**: Validate `AccountId` joins correctly with `Account.Id`.
- **Opportunity**: Handle `amount` and `closedate` parsing carefully.
- **Project__c**: Ensure `Account__c` and `Opportunity__c` reference the correct Salesforce-style `Id` fields.
- **Installed_Asset__c**: Validate `Account__c` and `Project__c` joins correctly with their respective tables.
Source tables: fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c
Join keys: account__c, opportunity__c
- Id <- id (Direct mapping from source `id`.)
- Name <- name (Use INITCAP(TRIM(name)). NOT NULL: fallback to 'Unknown' if NULL.)
- Project_Status__c <- project_status__c (Normalize to enum: 'Active', 'Completed', 'In Planning', 'On Hold', 'Cancelled'. Use INITCAP(TRIM()) and map variants. Fallback to NULL.)
- Go_Live_Date__c <- go_live_date__c (Parse source `go_live_date__c` using TO_DATE with format guards. Output as ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text. Fallback to NULL if unparseable.)
- Account__c <- account__c (Direct mapping from source `account__c`. Join with `Account.Id` to ensure consistency.)
- Opportunity__c <- opportunity__c (Direct mapping from source `opportunity__c`. Join with `Opportunity.Id` to ensure consistency.)
- Legacy_Project_ID__c <- id (Use source `id` as the legacy project ID.)
- CreatedDate <- (unspecified) (Use CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text for all rows.)
- LastModifiedDate <- (unspecified) (Use CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text for all rows.)
- IsDeleted <- (unspecified) (Default to 0 (false).)
Notes: `Account__c` and `Opportunity__c` must reference the Salesforce-style `Account.Id` and `Opportunity.Id` respectively. Enum normalization is applied to `Project_Status__c`.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

      WHEN warranty_end_date__c ~ '^\d{2}-\d{2}-\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(warranty_end_date__c, 'DD-MM-YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')

  CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::text AS "CreatedDate",

  0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM parsed_dates
